title: USS Nimitz Encounter
subtitle: The Tic Tac Incident
location: Pacific Ocean, ~100mi SW of San Diego
heroImageAlt: FLIR1 infrared video still showing the Tic Tac object

timeline.title: Anomalous radar contacts begin
timeline.detail: USS Princeton’s AN/SPY-1B radar detects objects at 80,000+ ft, descending to sea level in seconds — rates exceeding ballistic missile profiles. Senior Chief Kevin Day tracks returns over two weeks.

timeline.title: Fravor & Dietrich vectored to contact
timeline.detail: CDR David Fravor and LCDR Alex Dietrich, flying two F/A-18F Super Hornets from VFA-41 "Black Aces," are diverted to a live radar return ~100 miles SW of San Diego.

timeline.title: Visual contact — Tic Tac above churning ocean
FLIR1 infrared video captured
timeline.detail: WSO Chad Underwood records 76 seconds of IR video. The footage shows a featureless oblong with no exhaust plume, rotating on its longitudinal axis while maintaining flight.

timeline.title: Pentagon confirms AATIP, releases FLIR1
timeline.detail: NYT publishes bombshell investigation. Pentagon ran a $22M secret UAP program. DoD releases FLIR1 after 13 years. Luis Elizondo resigns AATIP in protest over secrecy.

timeline.title: Congressional hearings & public testimony
timeline.detail: Fravor testifies under oath. Dietrich on 60 Minutes. Kevin Day speaks publicly. AARO is established. The Nimitz encounter becomes the most cited UAP case in legislative history.

evidence.title: AN/SPY-1B Radar Tracking
evidence.summary: Princeton’s AEGIS radar tracked multiple objects at 80,000+ ft descending to sea level in seconds. Radar operator Kevin Day tracked returns over ~2 weeks before the intercept.
evidence.linkText: 🔗 Linked to: FLIR1 Video, Fravor Testimony, 60-Mile Relocation
evidence.imageAlt: AN/SPY-1B phased-array radar panels on a Ticonderoga-class cruiser

evidence.title: FLIR1 Infrared Video
evidence.summary: 76 seconds recorded, 35 publicly released. Zero exhaust plume — thermodynamically impossible for any known propulsion. Object rotates on longitudinal axis while maintaining trajectory.
evidence.linkText: 🔗 Linked to: SPY-1 Radar, Underwood Testimony, SCU Analysis
evidence.imageAlt: FLIR1 infrared still showing the Tic Tac object

evidence.title: 60-Mile Instant Relocation
evidence.summary: Object reappears at the fighters’ CAP point — a pre-assigned coordinate known only to Navy personnel. Implied speed ~46,000 mph. No sonic boom detected.
evidence.linkText: 🔗 Linked to: SPY-1 Radar, CAP Point Awareness

evidence.title: Ocean Surface Disturbance
evidence.summary: Cross-shaped area of churning whitewater the size of a 737. Calm surrounding seas. No submarine or surface vessel detected. Disturbance ceased with Tic Tac departure.
evidence.linkText: 🔗 Linked to: Water Pattern (Shag Harbour), Subsurface Component

witnesses.name: CDR David Fravor
witnesses.observed: Observed the Tic Tac at close range. Attempted to intercept. Object mirrored his descent, then accelerated instantaneously. 18 years experience, 3,500+ flight hours, Top Gun graduate.
witnesses.quote: “It accelerated like nothing I’ve ever seen.”

witnesses.name: LCDR Alex Dietrich
witnesses.observed: Observed from higher altitude. Independently corroborated all details — ocean disturbance, mirroring behavior, instantaneous departure. First female fighter pilot to speak publicly.
witnesses.quote: “I don’t identify it as any kind of aircraft that I know.”

witnesses.name: Sr. Chief Kevin Day
witnesses.observed: First to identify anomalous radar returns. Tracked objects over two weeks. Personally vectored Fravor’s flight. Later reported cognitive effects post-encounter. 20+ years Navy, Top Secret clearance.

aiAnalysis.title: Cross-Corpus: Water Disturbance Pattern
aiAnalysis.finding: Water interaction observed in 3 other high-credibility cases: Shag Harbour 1967 (physical traces), Aguadilla 2013 (thermal video), Catalina Channel 1958 (military witnesses). P(coincidence) < 0.3%.

aiAnalysis.title: Acceleration Analysis: 75g – 12,600g
aiAnalysis.finding: SCU estimates 75–5,000g based on FLIR1 tracking. Alternative radar calculation yields ~12,600g. Human tolerance: 9g. F-35 structural limit: 13.5g. Orders of magnitude beyond any known technology.

aiAnalysis.title: 13-Year Disclosure Gap
aiAnalysis.finding: Belgian Wave: 20 years to acknowledgment. Tehran 1976: decades via FOIA. Nimitz: 13 years. Pattern suggests deliberate institutional delay, not routine bureaucratic process.

openQuestions.text: What is in the classified annex of the AARO Historical Report Vol. 1?
openQuestions.context: The public report calls Nimitz "among the most significant unresolved cases" but the annex remains sealed.

openQuestions.text: Where are the original high-resolution FLIR recordings?
openQuestions.context: Only a downgraded copy survived. Multiple crew members describe confiscation by unidentified individuals.

openQuestions.text: Was the object aware of the fighters’ CAP point?
openQuestions.context: It reappeared at a pre-assigned coordinate known only to Navy personnel.

openQuestions.text: What caused the ocean surface disturbance?
openQuestions.context: Cross-shaped churning whitewater the size of a 737. No submarine detected. Suggests a subsurface component.

openQuestions.text: What does USS Louisville’s sonar data show?
openQuestions.context: Los Angeles-class submarine was part of the strike group. Sonar data status unknown.

resolution.label: Weather balloon
resolution.detail: Eliminated. Object tracked at hypersonic speeds. No balloon matches observed flight profile.

resolution.label: Conventional aircraft
resolution.detail: Eliminated. No wings, no exhaust, no IFF transponder. Flight profile exceeds any known airframe.

resolution.label: Military drone / UAS
resolution.detail: Eliminated. No known 2004-era drone matches observed performance.

resolution.label: Astronomical object
resolution.detail: Eliminated. Object at low altitude, hovering and maneuvering. Broad daylight.

resolution.label: Sensor malfunction / radar clutter
resolution.detail: Partially addressed. SPY-1 team ruled out clutter. Visual confirmation supports physical object.

resolution.label: Hoax / fabrication
resolution.detail: Eliminated. Multiple independent witnesses, government-released video, congressional testimony under oath.

resolution.label: Classified US program
resolution.detail: Not fully eliminated. AARO found "no evidence" linking UAP to classified programs. Capabilities exceed known physics.

resolution.label: Foreign adversary technology
resolution.detail: Not fully eliminated. 20 years later no nation has demonstrated remotely comparable capabilities.

osint.value: SOCAL OPAREA, ~100mi SW of San Diego. 31.33°N, 117.17°W.
osint.source: Confirmed via USS Nimitz deployment records, FOIA documents.

osint.value: Clear skies, calm seas, excellent visibility. No clouds below 25,000 ft.
osint.source: NOAA historical weather data, all witness accounts.
Navy deployment schedule, USS Nimitz cruise book.

osint.value: Fravor: CO VFA-41, Top Gun grad, 3,500+ hrs. Dietrich: VFA-41. Day: 20+ yrs Navy.
osint.source: Navy records, congressional testimony, public interviews.

osint.value: Officially released by DoD. Metadata consistent with AN/ASQ-228 ATFLIR.
osint.source: DoD official release, FOIA confirmation, SCU analysis.

osint.value: Two crew members describe men arriving by helicopter to collect data.
osint.source: Documentary interviews. No official records or chain of custody found.

osint.value: USS Louisville (SSN-724) was part of CSG-11. Sonar data status unknown.
osint.source: No public records. FOIA not yet filed.

evidenceChain.note: Original tapes confiscated. Exists now only as verbal testimony.
Original confiscated. Downgraded copy survived. Officially released by DoD Dec 2017.
Independent account. 60 Minutes (May 2021). Corroborates all key details.
Radar operator. Public 2019. Describes 2 weeks of anomalous contacts.
PO Hughes and PO Voorhis. Documentary interviews only.

nextSteps.text: File FOIA for USS Louisville sonar logs (Nov 2004)
File FOIA for AARO Historical Report classified annex
Cross-reference NUFORC database for Nov 2004 SOCAL sightings
Analyze satellite imagery archives for ocean disturbance
Contact Jim Slaight (WSO, no public statements)

searchDemoResults.title: Frame-by-frame FLIR1 analysis reveals rotation axis anomaly
searchDemoResults.snippet: User /u/SkyWatcher2004 isolates 3 frames showing the object’s rotation changes direction mid-flight — inconsistent with any ballistic trajectory or known propulsion...

searchDemoResults.source: SCU · Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies
searchDemoResults.title: 2019 FLIR1 Reconstruction: Estimated acceleration 75g–5,400g
searchDemoResults.snippet: Detailed frame-by-frame reconstruction of the FLIR1 footage using calibrated lens parameters, atmospheric correction, and triangulation from known aircraft position...

searchDemoResults.title: David Fravor: Nimitz encounter full testimony (2h14m)
searchDemoResults.snippet: Commander Fravor’s most detailed account. Timestamps: 0:14:22 first visual, 0:31:05 mirroring behavior, 0:45:18 departure speed, 1:12:00 hard drive confiscation...

searchDemoResults.title: AATIP Program Documents — Nimitz Case References
searchDemoResults.snippet: Collection of declassified documents from the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program. Three memos reference the 2004 Nimitz encounter specifically...

searchDemoResults.source: Web · The War Zone / The Drive
searchDemoResults.title: Navy Confirms USS Louisville Was Part of Nimitz Strike Group
searchDemoResults.snippet: FOIA response confirms SSN-724 Louisville was operating with CSG-11 during November 2004. Sonar data status: "responsive records not located" — which may indicate classified hold...

investigationTasks.title: FLIR1 Official Video — Still Frame Extraction
investigationTasks.description: Download the FLIR1 video from NAVAIR FOIA Reading Room. Extract 4 key still frames: (1) first acquisition of Tic Tac, (2) object rotation on longitudinal axis, (3) object centered in frame showing oblong shape, (4) moment of apparent lock loss.
investigationTasks.attribution: U.S. Department of Defense / Naval Air Systems Command
investigationTasks.notes: US Government work. No copyright restrictions. The most important images in the entire project.

investigationTasks.title: GIMBAL & GOFAST — Still Frame Extraction
investigationTasks.description: Download GIMBAL and GOFAST videos from NAVAIR FOIA. Extract 1 representative frame from each. These are USS Roosevelt (Case 006) but released alongside FLIR1 and cross-referenced in this case.
investigationTasks.attribution: U.S. Department of Defense / Naval Air Systems Command
investigationTasks.notes: Cross-case reference images. GIMBAL shows rotation. GOFAST shows speed against ocean background.

investigationTasks.title: SCU Forensic Analysis — Key Diagrams
investigationTasks.description: Extract 3-4 key figures from the 270-page SCU report: trajectory reconstruction, acceleration estimation charts, radar track visualization, and the comparative g-force diagram.
investigationTasks.notes: Academic publication by Knuth, Powell, Reali. Cite properly. Peer-reviewed in Entropy (MDPI).

investigationTasks.title: SOCAL OPAREA Map — Encounter Location
investigationTasks.description: Create or source a map showing the Southern California Operating Area with the approximate encounter location (~100mi SW of San Diego). Include the positions of USS Nimitz, USS Princeton, and the intercept point.
investigationTasks.notes: OpenStreetMap base is CC-BY-SA. Could also use NOAA nautical chart (public domain).

investigationTasks.title: AN/SPY-1B Radar System — Reference Photo
investigationTasks.description: Source a photo of the AN/SPY-1B radar arrays on a Ticonderoga-class cruiser. These are the octagonal panels visible on the superstructure. US Navy photos of commissioned ships are public domain.
investigationTasks.notes: Any official US Navy photo of USS Princeton or similar Ticonderoga-class cruiser showing the SPY-1 arrays.

investigationTasks.title: F/A-18F Super Hornet — Reference Photo
investigationTasks.description: Source a US Navy photo of an F/A-18F Super Hornet from VFA-41 'Black Aces' if possible, or any Super Hornet. This is the aircraft type flown by Fravor and Dietrich.
investigationTasks.notes: VFA-41 Black Aces specific livery preferred. Any F/A-18F acceptable.

investigationTasks.title: Fravor Congressional Testimony — Document Scan
investigationTasks.description: Download Fravor's written statement to the House Oversight Committee (July 26, 2023). Screenshot or scan the first page for display as a document card.
investigationTasks.attribution: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability
investigationTasks.notes: Congressional testimony is public record. The document itself is part of the official government record.

gallery.title: FLIR1 — "Tic Tac" Infrared Video
gallery.caption: 76-second infrared video captured by LT Chad Underwood's ATFLIR pod. Shows acquisition, tracking, object rotation, and apparent lock break. The only declassified sensor footage from the 2004 Nimitz encounter.

gallery.title: FLIR1 — First Acquisition
gallery.caption: First clear acquisition of the Tic Tac object by the ATFLIR pod.

gallery.title: FLIR1 — Object Rotation
gallery.caption: The object rotates on its longitudinal axis — a behavior inconsistent with any known aircraft or ballistic trajectory.

gallery.title: FLIR1 — Tic Tac Shape
gallery.caption: Object centered in frame showing the characteristic elongated oval ("Tic Tac") shape. No visible wings, exhaust, or control surfaces.

gallery.title: FLIR1 — Lock Loss
gallery.caption: Final moments of the FLIR1 footage. The ATFLIR pod appears to lose lock on the object as it accelerates beyond tracking capability.

gallery.title: GIMBAL — Rotating Object (2015)
gallery.caption: USS Roosevelt encounter, 2015. Object appears to rotate against the wind. Cross-case reference — officially released alongside FLIR1.

gallery.title: GOFAST — High-Speed Object (2015)
gallery.caption: USS Roosevelt encounter, 2015. Object tracks at high speed against ocean background. Cross-case reference.

gallery.title: AN/SPY-1B Radar Arrays — Ticonderoga-class Cruiser
gallery.caption: The AN/SPY-1B phased-array radar system aboard USS Princeton tracked anomalous contacts for two weeks before the visual encounter. These octagonal panels provide 360° coverage.

gallery.title: F/A-18F Super Hornet — VFA-41 Black Aces
gallery.caption: The aircraft type flown by CDR David Fravor and LCDR Alex Dietrich during the intercept. VFA-41 'Black Aces' were embarked on USS Nimitz.

gallery.title: SCU Analysis — Trajectory Reconstruction
gallery.caption: Forensic trajectory reconstruction from the 2019 SCU peer-reviewed analysis (Knuth, Powell, Reali). Published in Entropy (MDPI).

gallery.title: SCU Analysis — Acceleration Estimates
gallery.caption: Estimated acceleration range: 75g to 5,400g depending on assumptions. Any value in this range exceeds known aerospace technology by orders of magnitude.

gallery.title: SCU Analysis — Radar Track Observations
gallery.caption: Radar data analysis from the AN/SPY-1B tracking data. Shows contact behavior patterns over the two-week observation period.

gallery.title: SCU Analysis — G-Force Comparison
gallery.caption: Comparison of estimated Tic Tac acceleration against known aircraft and spacecraft capabilities. The Tic Tac's performance exceeds every reference vehicle.

gallery.title: CDR Fravor — Congressional Testimony (Page 1)
gallery.caption: Written statement submitted to the House Oversight Committee, July 26, 2023. Fravor's sworn account of the November 14, 2004 encounter.
gallery.attribution: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability

gallery.title: SOCAL OPAREA — Encounter Location Map
gallery.caption: Reconstructed encounter map showing the W-291 operating area, CSG-11 vessel positions, Fravor's intercept path, and the Tic Tac encounter point ~100nm SW of San Diego.

verifiedFacts.fact: AN/SPY-1B radar aboard USS Princeton tracked anomalous returns for approximately two weeks prior to November 14, 2004.
sources: Senior Chief Kevin Day — sworn congressional testimony (2023)
USS Princeton radar operators — multiple corroborating accounts
SCU forensic analysis of radar data characteristics

verifiedFacts.fact: Four Navy pilots across two F/A-18F Super Hornets made independent visual contact with a white, oblong object on November 14, 2004.
sources: CDR David Fravor — congressional testimony under oath (July 2023)
LCDR Alex Dietrich — 60 Minutes interview (May 2021)
DoD official acknowledgment (April 2020 video release)
verifiedFacts.relatedTimeline: Visual contact — Tic Tac above churning ocean

verifiedFacts.fact: The FLIR1 infrared video is 76 seconds long and was captured by an ATFLIR pod on an F/A-18F flown by LT Chad Underwood (VFA-41).
sources: U.S. Department of Defense — official release (April 2020)
NAVAIR FOIA Reading Room
LT Chad Underwood — interviews confirming he captured the footage

verifiedFacts.fact: The encounter occurred approximately 100 nautical miles southwest of San Diego in the W-291 SOCAL Operating Area.
sources: USS Nimitz CSG-11 operational records
SCU geographic reconstruction
Multiple pilot testimonies confirming SOCAL OPAREA

verifiedFacts.fact: Radar data tapes and FLIR recordings were collected from USS Princeton by unidentified personnel after the encounter.
sources: PO Patrick 'PJ' Hughes — testimony about data collection
CDR David Fravor — congressional testimony
Multiple Princeton crew corroborating accounts

verifiedFacts.fact: The object demonstrated no visible propulsion: no exhaust plume, no wings, no control surfaces, no rotor.
sources: CDR Fravor — direct observation at close range
LCDR Dietrich — direct observation
FLIR1 IR video — no thermal exhaust signature visible

verifiedFacts.fact: USS Princeton's radar recorded the object relocating from the intercept point to Fravor's CAP point (~60 miles) in seconds.
sources: Senior Chief Kevin Day — congressional testimony
USS Princeton CIC personnel
verifiedFacts.disputedBy: Exact speed calculation disputed — SCU estimates range from 46,000 mph (instantaneous) to lower bounds depending on radar sampling rate assumptions.

verifiedFacts.fact: No sonic boom was detected or reported despite apparent hypersonic speed.
sources: CDR Fravor — explicit statement
No Princeton crew reported sonic boom
No seismic or acoustic records of sonic event

verifiedFacts.fact: Weather on November 14, 2004 was clear with calm seas — 'a perfect day for flying' (Fravor).
sources: NOAA historical weather records
Multiple pilot descriptions
Clear-sky conditions visible in FLIR1 ocean background

verifiedFacts.fact: CDR David Fravor testified under oath before the U.S. House Oversight Committee on July 26, 2023.
sources: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability — official record
C-SPAN broadcast
Written statement available from oversight.house.gov

hypothesisProbabilities.reasoning: The only hypothesis with zero inconsistencies in the ACH matrix. Consistent with all sensor data (radar, IR, visual), the absence of propulsion signatures, and the extreme performance characteristics (est. 75g–5,400g acceleration). No known technology matches the observed behavior.

Would require simultaneous failure of AN/SPY-1B radar, ATFLIR infrared pod, AND visual perception of four trained Navy pilots. Multi-platform, multi-sensor confirmation makes systemic error highly unlikely. The 2-week radar tracking period further reduces random error probability.

No known drone in 2004 (or 2026) can hover without visible propulsion, accelerate to hypersonic speeds without sonic boom, or match the physical description. However, an unknown advanced drone program cannot be fully excluded.

Clear weather conditions (VF-009), direct visual contact at close range by trained observers, and coherent radar/IR signatures eliminate atmospheric phenomena (mirage, temperature inversion, ball lightning).

No conventional aircraft can operate without visible propulsion, hover silently, accelerate instantaneously, or match the described Tic Tac morphology. Fravor — a career fighter pilot — explicitly stated it was not any aircraft he had ever seen.

researchBranches.question: How fast was the Tic Tac actually traveling during the 60-mile relocation?
researchBranches.anchorText: USS Princeton's radar recorded the object relocating from the intercept point to Fravor's CAP point (~60 miles) in seconds.

findings.content: SCU peer-reviewed analysis (Knuth, Powell, Reali, 2019) estimates acceleration between 75g and 5,400g. Lower bound assumes maximum radar sampling interval. Upper bound assumes instantaneous acceleration.

Skeptic Mick West argues the object may have been at a different range than assumed, which would reduce speed estimates. However, this interpretation requires the AN/SPY-1B radar range data to be fundamentally misread — which contradicts Senior Chief Day's testimony about confirmed track correlation.

The SPY-1B refreshes approximately every 6 seconds in track-while-scan mode. If the Tic Tac moved 60 miles between two consecutive scans: 60 miles / 6 seconds = 36,000 mph (~Mach 47). If it took two scan cycles (12 seconds): 18,000 mph (~Mach 23). All scenarios exceed any known aerospace technology by orders of magnitude.

researchBranches.question: What was the cross-shaped water disturbance, and what could create that pattern?
researchBranches.anchorText: Both crews spot a cross-shaped disturbance in the water — whitewater the size of a 737.

findings.content: Fravor described the disturbance as looking like 'something was just below the surface.' The Tic Tac was hovering approximately 50 feet above this disturbance. When Fravor descended to investigate, the Tic Tac appeared to respond to his approach.

researchBranches.question: Who collected the data tapes from USS Princeton, and where did they go?
researchBranches.anchorText: Radar data tapes and FLIR recordings were collected from USS Princeton by unidentified personnel after the encounter.

findings.content: PO Patrick 'PJ' Hughes stated that two men in plain clothes with no unit patches arrived by helicopter, asked for the radar data tapes, and left. No receipts were provided. No chain of custody documentation exists.
findings.source: PJ Hughes — multiple interviews and documentary appearances

findings.content: The DoD has not confirmed or denied this data collection event. FOIA requests for chain-of-custody records have returned 'no responsive documents' — which could mean the records don't exist or are classified.
